reasoning_model_1: The provided output for Model 1 consists of the text string 'This is my last warning to you.' rather than an audio file. As the output is text-based, it completely fails to convey the required paralinguistic audio features. Specifically, for the dimension of 'Pace', there is no acoustic data to demonstrate a 'fast pace'; the text merely represents the semantic content. Furthermore, the punctuation used is a period ('.') rather than the exclamation mark ('!') in the prompt, implying a neutral declarative tone rather than the urgent speed requested. For 'Stress', there is no auditory signal to emphasize the word 'is'. Consequently, the model fails to generate audio and thus fails all paralinguistic constraints.
reasoning_model_2: The provided output for Model 2 is also the text string 'This is my last warning to you.' without any accompanying audio data. Similar to Model 1, the absence of an audio waveform makes it impossible to perceive or evaluate the requested paralinguistic features. The text output cannot exhibit a 'fast pace' or specific 'stress' on the word 'is'. Following the evaluation criterion, if the generated audio is missing or the content is not audio (empty/text-only implies extremely poor audio generation performance), the score must be 0. The output fails to meet the specific acoustic requirements of the prompt.
model_comparison: Both models failed to generate valid audio files and instead provided text transcripts. As a result, neither model could demonstrate the requested paralinguistic features of 'Pace' (Fast) and 'Stress' (on the word 'is'). Since text outputs lack acoustic properties entirely, both models are non-compliant with the audio generation task. They are tied with a score of 0 across all dimensions due to the failure to produce audio content.
